I want to just talk it out with my ex-boyfriend, but I don't know if that's a good idea because of the possibility that it could blow up in my face. If he told his girlfriend, everything could go wrong because she hates me. If my current boyfriend knew, he would be crushed. On one hand, I want my ex-boyfriend to say no to having feelings for me, but I would be terrified of what to do if he said he has feelings too. Where would I go from there? On the other hand, I feel like I can't move forward in my relationship with my current boyfriend without closure from this. Sometimes I wish I could just cut off everything that had to do with my ex, but I don't want to. Please help me, because I really have no idea what to do.
response:
This is a difficult situation, and I can hear how much mental energy this is taking from you. Let me try to help you think through what's really happening here.
What you're describing isn't actually about needing closure. You had closure — the relationship ended years ago, you both moved on to other people, you tried a casual reconnection and you ended it. Closure happened. What you're experiencing now is an attraction to the fantasy of what might have been, amplified by sexual tension that's never been resolved and the forbidden nature of the situation.
The fact that you're "so happy" with your current boyfriend but simultaneously obsessing about your ex suggests something important: this isn't really about choosing between two people. If it were, you'd have clearer dissatisfaction with your current relationship. Instead, this seems more about an unresolved "what if" that feels safe to fantasize about precisely because it's unavailable.
Here's what I think you need to consider:
Talking to your ex won't give you closure — it will create chaos. You already know this. You're hoping he'll either shut it down (so you can stop wondering) or validate your feelings (which terrifies you because you don't know what you'd do). But there's a third, more likely outcome: an ambiguous conversation that leaves you more confused, potentially damages multiple relationships, and still doesn't resolve the obsession.
The "closure" you need isn't from him — it's a decision you make yourself. You need to decide whether you're truly committed to your current relationship. Because right now, you're keeping one foot out the door by maintaining this emotional energy around your ex. That's not fair to your boyfriend, even if he doesn't know about it.
Some hard questions to sit with:

Are you fully satisfied in your current relationship, or is something missing that makes the fantasy of your ex appealing?
Is the pull toward your ex actually about him, or about reclaiming a time in your life that felt more exciting, less settled, more unknown?
Can you genuinely imagine a real, day-to-day relationship with your ex, or are you attracted to the idea of him precisely because it's incomplete?

What I'd suggest:

Create distance from your ex. Not because he's bad, but because proximity is feeding the obsession. You can't get over something you're actively watering.
Talk to a therapist. Not your ex, not your boyfriend yet — a neutral third party who can help you understand what this fixation is really about.
Make a choice about your current relationship. If you decide you want to stay, you need to fully commit — which means letting go of the "what if" with your ex. If you realize you're not fully in it, that's important information too, but it should lead to an honest conversation with your boyfriend, not to pursuing your ex.

The racing thoughts, the inability to nap, the years-long preoccupation — this has become an anxiety loop, not a legitimate question about who to be with. You need to break the loop, not feed it by seeking a conversation that promises resolution but will likely just add more variables.
What do you think is really driving this? Is there something missing in your current relationship, or is this more about not wanting to let go of a particular story about yourself and your past?

question: How can my boyfriend forgive me when I can't forgive myself? I have been in a relationship with my boyfriend for about six years now. In the past, our relationship was difficult and frustrating. We argued a lot, and due to that, there was a lot of tension between us. We stayed together because we love each other and wanted to make it work. I used to party a lot, and several times I got into situations where I would end up kissing someone else. These situations were never more than just kissing. I have come clean about these situations with my boyfriend, and he decided to forgive and move forward with me. I love him so much and want to work things out too, but I'm having a difficult time understanding how he can forgive me. I can't seem to forgive myself. I'm overwhelmed with feelings of guilt and unworthiness.
response: It's normal to feel guilty after hurting someone you care about. Have you talked to your boyfriend about how you're feeling? It's possible that he has forgiven you, but that you're still holding on to the guilt. It might help to talk to him about it and hear his perspective. Maybe he sees things differently than you do.

question: How can my boyfriend forgive me when I can't forgive myself? I have been in a relationship with my boyfriend for about six years now. In the past, our relationship was difficult and frustrating. We argued a lot, and due to that, there was a lot of tension between us. We stayed together because we love each other and wanted to make it work. I used to party a lot, and several times I got into situations where I would end up kissing someone else. These situations were never more than just kissing. I have come clean about these situations with my boyfriend, and he decided to forgive and move forward with me. I love him so much and want to work things out too, but I'm having a difficult time understanding how he can forgive me. I can't seem to forgive myself. I'm overwhelmed with feelings of guilt and unworthiness.
response: Hi Cerritos,This is an interesting twist because it's more common for the person in your position to want to move forward, and for the person who was wronged to struggle with forgiveness. You are lucky to have a loving, compassionate partner, and your boyfriend is lucky to have a partner who takes full responsibility for their actions. My hunch is that you learned some things about yourself when you were younger that are playing a role here. Your sense of worthlessness seems out of proportion to the mistakes you made. You don't have to be perfect in order to deserve the love of a good man. You only have to have the maturity to recognise when you've hurt someone and work hard to make it better. Who in your life overreacted to small mistakes you made? Were you shamed as a child? Did you learn that you deserved to be punished? Did something bad happen that you thought was your fault? Is there a mistake you made long ago that you need forgiveness for? There is a younger person inside you waiting to be forgiven for something they weren't entirely responsible for. The bar is too high for you. If I was your therapist, I would work with you to find the source of the shame, and address that wound. If you want to move forward and be with your boyfriend, your job will be to forgive yourself. Forgiving doesn't mean "it was okay"; forgiving simply means that it happened, that you can't erase it, and that you don't want to carry it around or punish yourself for it anymore. You have done many things here that you can feel proud of! You've 'come clean', you've been honest, you've taken responsibility for your actions, you've not tried to minimize what you did, and you've chosen to be more loyal and aware of how you impact your boyfriend. These are all things you can use to build your sense of worth. You are acting very honourably. It's time to put your past mistakes away on the shelf knowing that you've learned from them and are a better person now. It's not our mistakes...not our worst moments that define us...it's how we handle them afterwards.I wish you growth and happiness.

question: How can my boyfriend forgive me when I can't forgive myself? I have been in a relationship with my boyfriend for about six years now. In the past, our relationship was difficult and frustrating. We argued a lot, and due to that, there was a lot of tension between us. We stayed together because we love each other and wanted to make it work. I used to party a lot, and several times I got into situations where I would end up kissing someone else. These situations were never more than just kissing. I have come clean about these situations with my boyfriend, and he decided to forgive and move forward with me. I love him so much and want to work things out too, but I'm having a difficult time understanding how he can forgive me. I can't seem to forgive myself. I'm overwhelmed with feelings of guilt and unworthiness.
response:
It is incredibly heavy to carry the weight of a version of yourself that you no longer recognize or like. It makes sense that you’re struggling; when we love someone deeply, the idea that we hurt them feels like a fundamental betrayal of who we want to be. 

However, by refusing to forgive yourself, you might accidentally be creating a new wall between you and the person you're trying to get closer to. Here is a perspective on why he can forgive you—and how you can start to do the same.

---

## 1. Trust His Autonomy
When you say, "I don't understand how he can forgive me," you are inadvertently questioning his judgment. It feels like humility, but it can actually be a form of disbelief in his emotional intelligence. 
* **He sees the whole timeline:** You are hyper-focused on the mistakes. He is looking at the **six-year arc**. He sees the growth, the honesty of your confession, and the person you are today.
* **Respect his choice:** If he says he forgives you, believe him. To keep punishing yourself is to suggest that he is wrong about his own feelings or that he’s "too nice" to know better. 

## 2. Separate Guilt from Shame
There is a vital distinction between these two emotions that might help you process this:
* **Guilt:** "I did something bad." (This is healthy; it’s your moral compass working).
* **Shame:** "I **am** bad." (This is toxic; it keeps you stuck in the past and makes you retreat).

Your boyfriend has forgiven the *action* (the guilt). You are still holding onto the *identity* (the shame). He isn't dating the person who made those mistakes in a vacuum; he is dating the person who had the courage to tell him the truth.

## 3. The "Penance" Trap
Sometimes we hold onto self-loathing because it feels like a form of payment. We think, *“If I feel bad enough for long enough, maybe I’ll have suffered as much as I made him suffer.”*
> **The Reality:** Your suffering doesn't heal him. Your **presence, consistency, and joy** do. By staying in a state of "unworthiness," you aren't actually making it up to him—you’re staying emotionally unavailable because you're too busy looking inward at your own guilt.

## 4. How to Practice Self-Forgiveness
Forgiveness isn't a one-time "reset" button; it’s a practice.
* **The "Friend" Test:** If your best friend told you this exact story about her relationship, would you tell her she is a monster who deserves to feel unworthy forever? Likely not. Try to extend that same grace to yourself.
* **Amendments vs. Apologies:** You’ve already apologized. Now, live the amendment. Every day that you are faithful, present, and loving is a "deposit" into the relationship that outweighs those "withdrawals" from years ago.
* **Accept the Complexity:** You can be a good person who did a messy thing. Humans are capable of both deep love and impulsive mistakes simultaneously.

---

Your boyfriend isn't looking for a perfect partner; he’s looking for the person who chose to be honest with him even when it was terrifying. He’s already opened the door for you to move forward. You just have to decide to walk through it with him.

How has your boyfriend been reacting lately when you express these feelings of guilt to him?

question: How can my boyfriend forgive me when I can't forgive myself? I have been in a relationship with my boyfriend for about six years now. In the past, our relationship was difficult and frustrating. We argued a lot, and due to that, there was a lot of tension between us. We stayed together because we love each other and wanted to make it work. I used to party a lot, and several times I got into situations where I would end up kissing someone else. These situations were never more than just kissing. I have come clean about these situations with my boyfriend, and he decided to forgive and move forward with me. I love him so much and want to work things out too, but I'm having a difficult time understanding how he can forgive me. I can't seem to forgive myself. I'm overwhelmed with feelings of guilt and unworthiness.
response:
The fact that you're struggling with this shows how much you've grown and how deeply you care about him and your relationship. What you're experiencing is actually very common when someone genuinely confronts harm they've caused.
Here's what might help you understand the gap between his forgiveness and yours:
He sees the whole picture differently than you do. When you look back, you're flooded with shame and see only those moments of betrayal. When he looks at you, he sees six years together, the person you are now, your honesty in coming clean, and your commitment to change. He's choosing to focus on your relationship's future, while you're stuck reliving the past.
Forgiveness isn't about saying it was okay — it's about choosing not to let those moments define everything. Your boyfriend has made that choice. He's decided that those mistakes don't outweigh what you mean to him or who you're becoming. That's a gift, but it's also his genuine assessment of the situation.
Your guilt, while painful, shows something important: you've changed. The person who can't forgive those actions is different from the person who did them. That's actually evidence you've grown, not that you're unworthy.
Some questions to sit with:

What would need to happen for you to believe you deserve his forgiveness?
Are you perhaps afraid that if you forgive yourself, you'll somehow become that person again?
What would he say if he knew you were still carrying this weight?

Moving forward practically:
Consider whether you might benefit from talking to a therapist about this. These overwhelming feelings of unworthiness can sometimes be about more than just the specific incidents — they can tap into deeper beliefs about yourself.
Also, have you talked to your boyfriend about this — about your struggle to accept his forgiveness? Sometimes hearing directly from him why he's chosen to stay and what he values in you now can help bridge that gap.
You can't undo the past, but you can honor his choice to move forward by actually moving forward with him — not by staying trapped in guilt, but by being the partner you want to be now.

question: How can my boyfriend forgive me when I can't forgive myself? I have been in a relationship with my boyfriend for about six years now. In the past, our relationship was difficult and frustrating. We argued a lot, and due to that, there was a lot of tension between us. We stayed together because we love each other and wanted to make it work. I used to party a lot, and several times I got into situations where I would end up kissing someone else. These situations were never more than just kissing. I have come clean about these situations with my boyfriend, and he decided to forgive and move forward with me. I love him so much and want to work things out too, but I'm having a difficult time understanding how he can forgive me. I can't seem to forgive myself. I'm overwhelmed with feelings of guilt and unworthiness.
response:
First of all, it’s really brave of you to open up about your feelings and reflect on what happened. Self-forgiveness can feel incredibly difficult, especially when you’re carrying guilt for hurting someone you love. But it’s important to remember that forgiveness—both from your boyfriend and yourself—is a process, not an instant decision.

Here are some steps and perspectives that might help you work through this:

1. Understand His Forgiveness
It can be hard to accept forgiveness when you don’t feel you deserve it. But your boyfriend’s forgiveness is a gift he’s consciously choosing to give, based on his love and willingness to move forward with you. Forgiveness doesn’t mean he’s ignoring the past—it means he values you and the relationship enough to focus on building something better together.

Trust that his actions are genuine. By forgiving you, he’s showing that he sees your remorse and your commitment to change. Accepting his forgiveness is an act of respect for the love he’s offering you.

2. Recognize Your Growth
You’ve already taken two major steps: acknowledging what happened and being honest with your boyfriend. These are signs of growth and maturity. The guilt you feel is a signal that you care deeply about him and that you’re holding yourself accountable. Use this as motivation to continue growing into the best version of yourself.

Ask yourself:

What have I learned from this experience?
How can I use that knowledge to be a better partner moving forward?
Focus on how far you’ve come, not just on the mistakes you made.

3. Practice Self-Compassion
You’re human, and humans make mistakes. What matters most is that you’re working to repair the damage and ensure it doesn’t happen again. Beating yourself up won’t undo the past—it will only keep you stuck in a cycle of shame.

Try this exercise:

Imagine a close friend came to you with the same story. Would you tell them they’re unworthy of love? Likely not. You’d remind them that they’re human, they’ve made amends, and they deserve a chance to move forward. Offer yourself the same kindness.
4. Separate Guilt from Shame
There’s a difference between guilt and shame:

Guilt says, “I made a mistake.”
Shame says, “I am a mistake.”
Guilt can be constructive—it can guide you toward making amends and learning from your actions. Shame, on the other hand, can trap you in a belief that you’re unworthy or irredeemable. Remind yourself that your mistakes don’t define your worth. You are more than the worst things you’ve done.

5. Communicate with Your Boyfriend
Share your struggles with your boyfriend if you haven’t already. Let him know that you’re grateful for his forgiveness but are having a hard time forgiving yourself. This can strengthen your bond and help him understand what you’re going through.

He might be able to reassure you in ways that remind you of why you’re worthy of his love. Sometimes, hearing it from the person you’ve hurt can be a powerful step toward healing.

6. Forgive Yourself as an Act of Love
Forgiving yourself doesn’t mean excusing your past actions—it means recognizing that you’re more than your mistakes. It’s also an act of love for your boyfriend. By forgiving yourself, you can show up for him fully in the present, without being weighed down by guilt or self-doubt.

Think of self-forgiveness as a way of honoring the love and trust he’s given you. It allows you to focus on building a stronger, healthier relationship rather than being stuck in the past.

7. Seek Professional Support if Needed
If your guilt feels overwhelming and you’re struggling to process it, consider talking to a therapist. They can help you explore the root of your guilt, work through feelings of unworthiness, and develop healthier ways to cope.

A therapist can also help you identify patterns or beliefs that might be holding you back from forgiving yourself and moving forward.

8. Focus on the Present and Future
The past can’t be changed, but the present and future are in your hands. Focus on showing your boyfriend—and yourself—that you’ve grown and are committed to building a loving, trustworthy relationship.

Ask yourself:

What kind of partner do I want to be moving forward?
How can I show my love and commitment in everyday actions?
By focusing on your actions now, you can create a sense of pride and self-respect that helps you let go of the guilt.

Final Thought:
Forgiving yourself is a journey. It takes time, patience, and self-compassion. But it’s possible. Remember that making mistakes doesn’t mean you’re unworthy of love—it means you’re human. Your boyfriend has chosen to forgive you because he sees your value and your effort. Now it’s time for you to see it too.

response: So wonderful to have a boyfriend who is able to understand and forgive you. What do you think that he sees in you that allows him to do that? Does he see someone who like other humans makes mistakes? Does he see someone who despite hurtful choices is a good person committed to being a loving respectful partner? The compassion and understanding that we can often extend to others can be difficult to extend to ourselves. We often hold very unrealistic and perfectionist expectations for ourselves which may even lead to our poor choices in the first place - as a ways of letting off steam or rebelling against our high standards. Regardless of the reasons, we all make mistakes and imperfection does not make someone less loveable - it just makes them human. If your boyfriend is able to start clean with you, there must be a reason. What you do from here on out will define the kind of partner you are to him. question: I weigh over 220 pounds and I am 16 years old About 3 years ago or so I was skinny, but I was still ugly. I really do want to change that but I've tried 3 times. Now can I try but don't stop. At school I get bullied about my weight and my ugliness and I have been bullied my whole life that I believe them. How can I stop thinking about them and don't let it get in my head? My parents said I don't weight that much but, they do think I am fat but, they tell me I am not. I told my parents that I need to go to a therapist, but they think I am fine and I think they don't want to deal with it.
response:
It takes a massive amount of courage to be this honest about how you’re feeling. First off, I want to tell you that what you’re going through—the bullying, the feeling of being "stuck," and the frustration with your parents—is incredibly heavy for a 16-year-old to carry alone. 

It makes total sense that you believe the bullies. When you hear the same negative things your whole life, they start to sound like "facts" instead of what they actually are: cruel opinions from people who are likely dealing with their own insecurities.

Here is a breakdown of how to handle the mental "noise," your physical goals, and the situation with your parents.

---

## 1. Dealing with the "Mental Noise"
The hardest part of change isn't the gym; it's the stuff between your ears. When you believe you are "ugly" or "worthless," your brain tries to protect you by making you quit—because if you "fail," it proves the bullies right.

* **The "Radio" Technique:** Think of the bullies’ voices as a static-filled radio station in the background. You can’t always turn it off, but you don't have to sit there and listen to the lyrics. When a thought like *"I'm ugly"* pops up, acknowledge it: *"Oh, there’s that thought again,"* then go back to what you were doing. Don't fight it; just don't feed it.
* **Separating Worth from Weight:** Your value as a human is a fixed thing. It doesn’t go up when the scale goes down. If you try to lose weight because you "hate" yourself, you’ll likely quit when things get hard. Try to shift to: *"I’m doing this because I deserve to feel better and move easier."*

## 2. Navigating Your Parents and Therapy
It sounds like your parents are trying to protect your feelings by downplaying your weight, but in doing so, they are accidentally making you feel unheard. 

* **Try a "Serious Sit-Down":** Instead of mentioning it in passing, ask to talk to them for 10 minutes without phones. 
* **Use "I" Statements:** Instead of "You guys don't care," try: *"I feel really overwhelmed and lonely at school. I’m being bullied, and it’s affecting my mental health. I need a professional to talk to so I can learn how to handle this stress."* * **School Resources:** If they still refuse, please go to your **school counselor**. They are trained for this, it’s free, and they can sometimes help bridge the gap between you and your parents regarding therapy.

## 3. Breaking the "Start-Stop" Cycle
You mentioned you’ve tried three times and stopped. That usually happens because people try to change *everything* at once (the "all-or-nothing" trap).

* **The Power of "Small":** Don't try to lose 50 pounds. Try to walk for 20 minutes today. Just today.
* **Focus on Protein and Water:** At 16, your body is still developing. Don't do "crash diets." Focus on drinking more water and eating more protein (chicken, beans, eggs). It keeps you full so you don't feel like you're starving.
* **Find Your "Why":** Write down how you want to *feel* (stronger, more energetic, less out of breath), not just how you want to look.

---

### A Note on "Ugliness"
"Ugly" is a word used by people who lack imagination. At 16, your face and body are still changing drastically. Many of the "cool" or "attractive" kids in high school peak early, while others "grow into" their looks in their 20s. 

You aren't a finished product yet. You are a work in progress.

**If you ever feel like things are getting too dark and you can't cope, please reach out to a crisis line. You can text HOME to 741741 (in the US/Canada) to talk to someone anonymously.**

Which part of your day feels the hardest right now—is it the time spent at school, or the time spent at home?

question: I weigh over 220 pounds and I am 16 years old About 3 years ago or so I was skinny, but I was still ugly. I really do want to change that but I've tried 3 times. Now can I try but don't stop. At school I get bullied about my weight and my ugliness and I have been bullied my whole life that I believe them. How can I stop thinking about them and don't let it get in my head? My parents said I don't weight that much but, they do think I am fat but, they tell me I am not. I told my parents that I need to go to a therapist, but they think I am fine and I think they don't want to deal with it.
response:
I'm really sorry you're feeling this way. It's tough to deal with bullying, self-doubt, and the feeling that your concerns aren't being fully understood. You deserve kindness, love, and support, and I want to help you take positive steps toward feeling better about yourself. Here's some advice:

1. Understand That Your Worth Is Not Defined by Others
People who bully often do it because of their own insecurities. What they say about you doesn't truly reflect who you are.
Remind yourself every day: My value isn’t based on what others think or say.
Write down qualities you like about yourself, even small ones. For example: "I’m kind," "I try hard," "I care about others." These matter far more than appearance.
2. Focus on Small, Achievable Goals for Your Health
If you want to work on your physical health, that's okay! Approach it in a way that feels positive, not punishing.

Start with small changes:

Drink more water.
Aim to move your body for 20–30 minutes a day (e.g., walking, dancing, or playing a sport you enjoy).
Add more fruits and vegetables to your meals.
Make it fun: Find a type of exercise or activity you enjoy—dancing, swimming, or even playing games that get you moving.

Be patient: Change takes time, and progress isn't always visible right away. Celebrate every small step.

3. Take Care of Your Mental Health
Journaling: Write down your feelings. It’s a great way to release emotions and track your progress.
Positive affirmations: Start each day by saying something kind to yourself. For example: "I deserve respect," or "I am working toward being the best version of myself."
Mindfulness or meditation: Apps like Headspace or Calm can help you manage stress and stay focused on the present moment.
4. Find Support
Talk to someone you trust: If your parents don’t fully understand, is there a teacher, school counselor, or another adult you feel comfortable talking to? Sometimes schools have support groups or resources for students who are being bullied.
Online communities: There are safe, supportive spaces online where people share similar struggles. Look for groups that focus on self-love, fitness, or mental health.
5. Stand Up to Negative Thoughts
Every time you catch yourself thinking something negative, challenge it. For example:
Negative thought: "I’m ugly."
Challenge: "I’m more than my appearance. I’m a kind and thoughtful person."
This takes practice, but over time, you can train your brain to be kinder to yourself.
6. Push for Therapy if You Feel It’s Necessary
If your parents don’t think therapy is needed, you could try explaining how much the bullying and self-doubt are affecting you. For example, say: "I’m struggling to feel good about myself, and I think talking to a professional could help me feel better and learn how to handle these feelings."
If they still don’t support it, look into free or low-cost resources at school or in your community. Many schools have counselors you can talk to for free.
7. Remember, You’re in Control of Your Future
You’ve already shown strength just by wanting to make a change. That’s a powerful step.
Growth takes time, but each day is an opportunity to work toward the person you want to become—both physically and emotionally.
8. You Are Not Alone
Many people have felt what you're feeling, and they’ve come out stronger. You can too.
You’re only 16—you have so much time to grow, heal, and discover how amazing you truly are.
If you ever feel overwhelmed, don’t hesitate to reach out to a hotline or trusted adult. You matter, and there are people who care about you and want to help. Keep going—you’re stronger than you think. 💛

question: I weigh over 220 pounds and I am 16 years old About 3 years ago or so I was skinny, but I was still ugly. I really do want to change that but I've tried 3 times. Now can I try but don't stop. At school I get bullied about my weight and my ugliness and I have been bullied my whole life that I believe them. How can I stop thinking about them and don't let it get in my head? My parents said I don't weight that much but, they do think I am fat but, they tell me I am not. I told my parents that I need to go to a therapist, but they think I am fine and I think they don't want to deal with it.
response:
I hear you, and I want you to know that what you're going through is really difficult. Being bullied hurts deeply, especially when it's been happening for so long that those cruel words start to feel like truth. But they're not truth—they're just cruelty.
About the bullying getting in your head:
It makes complete sense that years of bullying would affect how you see yourself. Our brains are wired to believe what we hear repeatedly, even when it's wrong. Here are some things that might help:

Name it when it happens: When a bullying voice pops up in your head, try to recognize "that's the bullying talking, not reality." Just noticing the difference can help.
Find one person who sees you differently: A teacher, coach, school counselor, family member, or even one friend. Sometimes one authentic connection can start to counter years of negativity.
Document the good: When something goes well or someone is kind to you, write it down. Your brain will try to dismiss these moments—don't let it.

About wanting therapy:
You're showing real maturity by recognizing you could benefit from talking to someone. If your parents aren't hearing you, here are other options:

School counselor: They're trained for this and it's free. You don't need parental permission to talk to them.
Keep asking your parents: Sometimes parents need to hear something multiple times before they understand how serious it is. You could try: "I've been asking about therapy because I'm really struggling. I need more help than you can give me, and that's okay—that's what therapists are for."
Other trusted adults: A coach, teacher, religious leader, or doctor might be able to help advocate for you.

About your weight and health:
First: Your weight doesn't determine your worth, and it definitely doesn't make you ugly. But if YOU want to make changes for how YOU feel—not because of bullies—that's valid too.
At 16, you're still growing. Working with a doctor would be ideal to make sure any changes you make are healthy for your developing body. If your parents won't take you to a therapist, maybe they'd take you to a regular doctor for a physical? Doctors can give real guidance and sometimes help parents understand when their kid needs additional support.
The most important thing:
You deserve kindness—from others and from yourself. The fact that you've tried to make changes three times shows strength, not weakness. You're reaching out for help, recognizing what you need, and trying to take care of yourself. That takes courage.
You're not fine—you said it yourself—and it's okay not to be fine. Keep advocating for yourself. Talk to that school counselor. You don't have to do this alone.

question: How do I cope with "never being good enough?" I'm always told I'm not good enough or trying hard enough. I put 100% in to every thing. I'm worn out, I've worked on all of my family relationships, I'm doing great in school, I'm kind to those in need and otherwise. I stick to my religious beliefs and leave room for mistakes and learn from others so I don't have to make as many and the ones I do I learn from. Somehow it's still not good enough and nothing I do works. Nothing has changed, and I feel hopeless. Any suggestions or ideas?
response: I'm sorry that you feel this way despite all you do and as hard as you try.  No wonder you're feeling worn out and hopeless!  If you're doing your best and it's still not good enough then it's reasonable to expect that you would end up feeling burnt out.  It's like there's no more fuel to keep your fire going!  I would encourage you to approach this with curiosity.  In what areas are you not feeling "good enough?"  What does "good enough" even mean?  How are you measuring your success?   Is it measured by what other people say, whether or not they approve, or what other people think?  Is it measured by your own expectations, expectations of a boss, a parent, or a partner?  Are they realistic and achievable expectations?  I once had a boss who always gave negative feedback and criticism.  I started to get to the point where I questioned my career and whether or not it was for me.  When I took a step back and checked the facts, I realized that the feeling of being "not good enough" was coming up mostly in my interactions with my boss.  When I found myself thinking of quitting, I realized that it might be helpful to try talking to her about it first.  As scary as it was, it made a huge difference.  I practiced the skills I teach my clients including constructive criticism and assertive communication.  I told her that as much as I appreciated her trying to help by giving me feedback, I found myself feeling very discouraged after our meetings.  I braced for her response, which actually came to me as a surprise.  She admitted that many people have told her the same thing, and that it wasn't me.  She said that it was her own "stuff" that she needed to work on.   After that I chose to focus on the evidence that I was doing well and that my job has meaning and value.  I realized that "good enough" is how I choose to define it.  I worked on letting myself be human and realizing that I am doing my best and that's good enough (and that doesn't mean I can't still learn and grow).  Giving yourself permission to be human and stop judging yourself so harshly creates space for learning and growing without burning out.  It also gives you a sense of peace because you're not trying to live up to unrealistic expectations.  It's saying "I'm okay as I am, regardless of what anyone says or thinks."  It's also saying "I'm doing my best and I can do better, try harder, and be more motivated to change."Bottom line is, if you check the facts, you're doing great in many ways.  So whether the "not good enough" message is coming from outside or within, try to replace it with another narrative.  Maybe something like, "I'm okay as I am, even if I make mistakes and I'm always learning."  If you're getting criticism from others, try talking to them about it.  Let them know how they can best support you.  Maybe that means celebrating your accomplishments with you- no matter how small.  Most likely they want what's best for you and don't realize how their actions are affecting you.  If they don't want what's best for you, then you might want to consider how you can filter them out of your life or set healthy boundaries with them.  Keep your head up and stay focused on your strengths.  The fact that you are reaching out for help shows that you're proactive and open-minded; hang in there and it will get better!

question: How do I cope with "never being good enough?" I'm always told I'm not good enough or trying hard enough. I put 100% in to every thing. I'm worn out, I've worked on all of my family relationships, I'm doing great in school, I'm kind to those in need and otherwise. I stick to my religious beliefs and leave room for mistakes and learn from others so I don't have to make as many and the ones I do I learn from. Somehow it's still not good enough and nothing I do works. Nothing has changed, and I feel hopeless. Any suggestions or ideas?
response:
I'm really sorry you're feeling this way. It can be deeply exhausting and painful to give so much of yourself and still feel like you're falling short, especially when you’re already striving to be the best version of yourself. Here are some suggestions that might help you cope with these feelings and rediscover a sense of self-worth and peace:

1. Recognize the Source of These Expectations
Ask yourself: Where is this "not good enough" message coming from?
Is it external (family, friends, society, or culture)?
Or is it internal (your own self-talk or inner critic)?
Sometimes, the people around us project their own insecurities or impossible standards onto us. Recognizing this can help you separate their expectations from what’s actually reasonable or true.
2. Redefine "Good Enough"
You’re pouring your heart into your actions, relationships, and beliefs. That is good enough.
Shift your focus from others’ approval to your own values. What does “being enough” look like to you? Are you kind, empathetic, and trying your best? If so, you are already succeeding.
3. Set Boundaries with Those Who Criticize
If family or others are constantly telling you that you’re not enough, it’s okay to set boundaries. You can say:
"I’m doing my best, and I need encouragement, not criticism."
Or, "I value your input, but I also need space to trust my own efforts."
Sometimes, people criticize because they think they’re helping, but you have the right to protect your emotional well-being.
4. Acknowledge Your Exhaustion
Feeling worn out is a sign that you’re doing too much and might be overextending yourself to meet others’ expectations. Prioritize rest and self-care:
Take breaks, even from helping others.
Give yourself permission to say no when you need to recharge.
You can’t pour from an empty cup, and taking care of yourself isn’t selfish—it’s necessary.
5. Replace "Hopeless" with "Curious"
When things feel stagnant and unchanging, it’s easy to feel hopeless. Instead, try shifting to curiosity:
"What small step could I take to nurture myself today?"
"What would my life look like if I focused on my own fulfillment instead of others’ approval?"
Curiosity can open doors to new perspectives and remind you that change is always possible, even if it’s slow.
6. Celebrate Your Wins
List your accomplishments, no matter how small. For example:
You’re doing well in school.
You’re kind to others.
You’re working on your relationships and learning from mistakes.
These are huge wins! Write them down and revisit them when self-doubt creeps in.
7. Surround Yourself with Supportive People
Seek out friends, mentors, or communities who affirm and encourage you. Sometimes, we need to be reminded that we’re valued just as we are.
If you don’t have that support in your current circle, consider joining groups (online or in-person) where people share your values and appreciate your kindness and effort.
8. Consider Professional Help
A therapist or counselor can provide a safe space to explore these feelings and help you reframe unhelpful thoughts. They can also teach coping strategies to manage the weight of expectations.
9. Practice Self-Compassion
Treat yourself with the same kindness and understanding you offer others. When you catch yourself thinking, "I’m not good enough," respond with:
"I’m doing my best, and that’s more than enough."
"I deserve love and kindness, even if I’m not perfect."
Self-compassion can be transformative and help quiet your inner critic.
10. Focus on What You Can Control
You can’t always control how others perceive or treat you, but you can control how you respond. Remind yourself:
"I can’t please everyone, and that’s okay."
"I have the power to define my own worth."
Final Thought
You are enough, simply because you exist—not because of what you achieve or how others see you. It’s okay to let go of perfection and focus on what makes you happy and fulfilled. Give yourself credit for all the hard work you’re doing, and know that it’s okay to rest and just be. You deserve kindness—from others, but most importantly, from yourself. ❤️

question: How do I cope with "never being good enough?" I'm always told I'm not good enough or trying hard enough. I put 100% in to every thing. I'm worn out, I've worked on all of my family relationships, I'm doing great in school, I'm kind to those in need and otherwise. I stick to my religious beliefs and leave room for mistakes and learn from others so I don't have to make as many and the ones I do I learn from. Somehow it's still not good enough and nothing I do works. Nothing has changed, and I feel hopeless. Any suggestions or ideas?
response:
I hear how exhausted and discouraged you're feeling. Pouring everything you have into your life and relationships, only to be told it's not enough, is genuinely painful—and it sounds like you've been carrying this weight for a while.
Here are some thoughts that might help:
Consider the source of "not good enough"
Who specifically is telling you this? Is it one person, multiple people, or has their voice become your own internal critic? Sometimes the people closest to us have their own unmet needs, insecurities, or impossible standards that have nothing to do with your actual worth or effort. Their dissatisfaction may reflect their issues, not your inadequacy.
The math isn't mathing
You describe someone who is: excelling academically, nurturing family relationships, living according to your values, showing kindness, and actively learning and growing. That's not someone who isn't "good enough"—that's someone who is thriving while being told they're failing. When reality and the message don't align, the message is the problem.
100% isn't sustainable (and shouldn't be the baseline)
Giving 100% to everything is a recipe for burnout, not success. Healthy, sustainable living involves seasons, priorities, and sometimes giving 70% to something so you can give 90% to something else. If nothing short of maximum effort counts as "trying," that's an unfair and unsustainable standard.
What would happen if you did less?
This is worth exploring: If you scaled back—not because you're giving up, but because you're testing the validity of these criticisms—what would actually happen? Sometimes we discover that the predicted catastrophe doesn't materialize, which reveals that the pressure was never about our performance.
You might be dealing with people who can't be satisfied
Some people move the goalposts. You meet their standard, so they raise it. You excel there, so they find a new metric. If you've genuinely addressed the issues raised and nothing has changed, you may be dealing with someone whose criticism serves a purpose for them (control, deflection of their own issues, maintaining a dynamic) that has nothing to do with you actually improving.
Redefine "enough"
What do you think is enough? Not as a defensive reaction, but genuinely—what does a life well-lived look like to you? Your description suggests you already have clear values. Are you living according to them? If yes, that might need to become your measuring stick, not someone else's chronic dissatisfaction.
Professional support could be valuable
Talking to a counselor or therapist could help you untangle whether you're dealing with unreasonable external voices, internalized criticism, or both—and develop strategies for setting boundaries and building resilience. Many schools offer free counseling services.
You mention leaving room for mistakes and learning from them—that's wisdom. But it also sounds like you might not be extending yourself the same grace you'd offer others. You're allowed to be a work in progress. You're allowed to be imperfect and still be enough.
The hopelessness you're feeling makes sense given the circumstances, but it's not a reflection of reality. Sometimes the answer isn't to do more—it's to protect yourself from voices that will never be satisfied, and to start listening to the truth: you're already doing enough. You already are enough.

question: How do I cope with "never being good enough?" I'm always told I'm not good enough or trying hard enough. I put 100% in to every thing. I'm worn out, I've worked on all of my family relationships, I'm doing great in school, I'm kind to those in need and otherwise. I stick to my religious beliefs and leave room for mistakes and learn from others so I don't have to make as many and the ones I do I learn from. Somehow it's still not good enough and nothing I do works. Nothing has changed, and I feel hopeless. Any suggestions or ideas?
response: Hello, and let me say first, what a great question! There are so many people putting forth their 100% everyday and often times, do not get the credit they deserve. Without being able to fully understand where you're question is coming from, I think there are a couple of different ways to look at this question.First, I think it's important for us to consider the message being sent by those in your life stating that you're "not good enough." Is that ACTUALLY what they said, or is this just what you heard? Sometimes, because of our own self-doubts and fears, we skew and misinterpret the messages we receive from others. Again, listen to the message being sent and attempt to remain objective. Is the message being sent coming from a place of love or concern? If so, have an honest and open communication with that individual about what your concerns are regarding the amount of energy and time you're spending on being "good enough." Explore with them how you feel you'r already giving your best.If the person is unwilling to work at understanding this concern you have, then it may be time to explore the relationship. Some relationships we engage in can be toxic, especially if the other person is toxic. It may be they have their own "stuff" going on and they feel the need to constantly put others down to make themselves feel better. If you're in the direct path of this individual, then it serves to reason you will probably be in the direct fire, as well. Explore whether or not you want this relationship to continue in your life. If it's a relationship that MUST continue, then work on what you will do to distance yourself from their toxicity or their problems. Until they're willing to work on these issues for themselves, then it may be necessary to find an exit strategy, such as leaving the room or simply refusing to engage in the conversation. Another perspective of this question is exploring the reason why it's important for YOU to satisfy the expectations of these other individuals? NO ONE will ever be 100% "good enough" for EVERYONE. Every single person has their own set of expectations in themselves and expectations in others. It would be impossible to satisfy all of those expectations for every single person in our lives. The question becomes, what is it WITHIN you that feels the need to meet these expectations? There is most likely a part of you, yourself, as an individual that feels "less than" and feels as if you, yourself, are not good enough. It then becomes impossible to every feel "good enough" for others, because it's really YOU you're trying to feel good enough for. If you already feel as if you're doing everything you can do to be at your best version of yourself, then really work on LETTING GO of the rest. No one is perfect, and striving for perfection in our lives only creates more anxiety and stress. Find a middle ground in which you are happy with your accomplishments and your daily goals, and learn to let go of what's left. After all, tomorrow is a new day and you can work on more of your goals tomorrow!I recommend doing this by finding a mantra, a statement or phrase you can repeat to yourself anytime you start to feel that sense of stress and pressure from striving for perfection. The mantra can remind you to let go of this expectation you have for yourself or that others have for you so you can also let go of the stress and anxiety surrounding the expectation. I leave you with one of my favorite quotes that perhaps you can use as your mantra, "No one can make you feel inferior without your consent. " - Eleanor Roosevelt.

question: How do I cope with "never being good enough?" I'm always told I'm not good enough or trying hard enough. I put 100% in to every thing. I'm worn out, I've worked on all of my family relationships, I'm doing great in school, I'm kind to those in need and otherwise. I stick to my religious beliefs and leave room for mistakes and learn from others so I don't have to make as many and the ones I do I learn from. Somehow it's still not good enough and nothing I do works. Nothing has changed, and I feel hopeless. Any suggestions or ideas?
response:
It is incredibly draining to pour $100\%$ of your soul into your life only to feel like you’re shouting into a void. First, I want to validate that what you’re feeling—that deep, bone-weary exhaustion—is a natural response to an impossible situation. You are essentially trying to fill a bucket that has a hole in the bottom.

If you are doing the work, showing up for others, excelling in school, and staying true to your values, the "not good enough" verdict isn't a reflection of your performance; it’s a reflection of the **unrealistic expectations** or **internal projections** of the people around you.

Here are a few ways to shift your perspective and protect your peace.

---

## 1. Internalize Your "Locus of Control"
In psychology, there is a concept called the **Locus of Control**. 
* **External Locus:** You believe your success or worth is defined by outside forces (parents, peers, critics).
* **Internal Locus:** You believe you are the architect of your own worth based on your effort and integrity.

Since you are already giving $100\%$, the "failure" isn't yours. You cannot control the "reception" of your efforts. If you do a perfect job and someone calls it "bad," the error is in their measurement, not your work.

## 2. Shift from "Seeking Approval" to "Seeking Alignment"
When we strive to be "good enough" for others, we are constantly chasing a moving goalpost. Instead, ask yourself: **"Am I in alignment with my own values?"**

| Goal | Seeking Approval | Seeking Alignment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Focus** | How do they feel about me? | How do I feel about my actions? |
| **Power** | Held by others. | Held by you. |
| **Outcome** | Anxiety and burnout. | Peace and self-respect. |

## 3. The Law of Diminishing Returns
You mentioned being "worn out." In economics and physics, there is a point where adding more effort doesn't produce better results—it just breaks the system.
* If you are giving $100\%$ and it isn't "enough," giving $110\%$ won't fix it either. It will only lead to resentment.
* **Idea:** Practice "Strategic Mediocrity" in areas that don't matter to you personally. Save that energy for your own mental health.

## 4. Re-evaluate the Sources
If the "never good enough" message is coming from specific people (like family), it’s important to realize that some people use criticism as a tool for **control** rather than a catalyst for your growth. 
* If they admit you are "good enough," they lose their leverage over you. 
* Recognizing this doesn't make it hurt less, but it helps you realize the "hopelessness" belongs to their character, not your future.

## 5. Lean Into Your Beliefs
You mentioned sticking to your religious beliefs. Most faiths emphasize that human perfection is impossible and that worth is inherent, not earned through a checklist of achievements. If your faith teaches grace, try to apply that same grace to yourself. If the Creator finds you worthy of existence, the opinions of tired, fallible humans are secondary.

---

> **A Small Suggestion:** > For one week, stop trying to improve your relationships or "be better." Just do the bare minimum requirements for school and work, and spend the rest of your time doing something that makes *you* feel capable, even if no one else sees it. Sometimes you have to "drop the rope" in the tug-of-war for approval just to see that you’re still standing without it.

Who is the person whose opinion carries the most weight in your head right now, and what would happen if you "fired" them from the job of judging you?

question: I'm addicted to smoking. How can I stop? I'm planning to have baby, so I have to quit smoking - but it's hard. Sometimes it's not a physical need, it's mental. I cannot help myself from thinking about smoking. What can I do to get rid of this addiction?

question: I'm addicted to smoking. How can I stop? I'm planning to have baby, so I have to quit smoking - but it's hard. Sometimes it's not a physical need, it's mental. I cannot help myself from thinking about smoking. What can I do to get rid of this addiction?
response: It's very admirable that you are trying to stop smoking for the sake of your health and your child's health.  The mental aspect of quitting a drug can and usually is the hardest part.  First, it can be helpful to change the focus of your thinking so you are focusing on the healthy behaviors you will start/improve rather than only focusing on the behavior that you are trying to stop.  The reason for this is that our brains hear the main topic of our thoughts.  That is, when you tell yourself "quit smoking, quit smoking, quit smoking", your brain hears "smoking, smoking, smoking".  So we need to put together a self-care plan that addresses your thinking, emotions, and behaviors (nutrition, physical activity, and other stress reducing activities).  The idea is to have an effective plan in place to both prevent yourself from feeling intense stress (which increases cravings) and to have a well-placed plan for when the intense cravings inevitably present themselves.  And lastly, and possibly the most important; You have to believe you deserve to be the healthiest version of yourself.  Think about the times when you are most vulnerable to smoke (stressful situations, after meals, when drinking, social situations, etc.).  The use of affirmations is also an important resource as what we say to ourselves effects our mood.  Repeatedly telling yourself "i am healthy, "i am getting healthier, "my lungs are becoming clear and healthy" or other affirmations like this.  Even if you don't believe them at first, continue saying them.  This step is important to improve cognitive flexibility which trains your brain to be open to change.  Think about activities that you like to do or that you would like to try and replace smoking with those activities.  Any activity that can make you laugh (time with friends, watching comedies, etc.) will evoke a calming response because when we feel happy, we typically don't feel stressed out simultaneously.

question: I'm addicted to smoking. How can I stop? I'm planning to have baby, so I have to quit smoking - but it's hard. Sometimes it's not a physical need, it's mental. I cannot help myself from thinking about smoking. What can I do to get rid of this addiction?
response:
I understand how challenging this is, especially with such an important reason to quit. The mental cravings you're describing are very real and often harder than the physical withdrawal. Here are some strategies that can help:
For the mental cravings:

Identify your triggers - Notice when the urge hits. Is it after meals, during stress, with coffee, or in social situations? Once you know your patterns, you can plan alternatives.
Delay and distract - When a craving hits, tell yourself you'll wait 10 minutes. Often the urge will pass. During that time, do something engaging - call someone, take a walk, drink water, or use a fidget toy.
Replace the ritual - Much of smoking is habitual behavior. Find a substitute ritual: deep breathing exercises, chewing gum, holding a stress ball, or drinking tea. The physical action can satisfy some of that mental need.
Change your self-talk - Instead of "I can't smoke," try "I don't smoke" or "I'm protecting my future baby." This shifts your identity rather than feeling deprived.

Practical support:

Consider nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) - Patches, gum, or lozenges can help with physical cravings while you work on the mental habit. Talk to your doctor about what's safe for pregnancy planning.
Get professional help - Your doctor can prescribe medications like varenicline (Chantix) or bupropion if appropriate. Quitlines (like 1-800-QUIT-NOW in the US) offer free coaching.
Build accountability - Tell people you're quitting. Join a support group or use apps like Smoke Free or QuitGuide that track progress and provide encouragement.

Timeline awareness:
The first 3 days are typically the hardest physically. Mental cravings can persist longer but become less intense and less frequent over weeks. Most people find significant relief after 2-4 weeks.
Since you're planning for pregnancy, quitting now is one of the best things you can do for your future baby's health - and your own. Would it help to talk through specific situations where you struggle most?

question: I'm addicted to smoking. How can I stop? I'm planning to have baby, so I have to quit smoking - but it's hard. Sometimes it's not a physical need, it's mental. I cannot help myself from thinking about smoking. What can I do to get rid of this addiction?
response: Quitting smoking can be difficult. It's also true that there is part that is sometimes a physical need and a part that is often connected to emotions in some way. From the way that you wrote this, it sounds like you may have been able to stop smoking physically, but still have cravings.One thing you can do is talk with your primary care physician or OB/GYN about whether there is anything that you can take to help with the cravings. Sometimes that can very helpful.As far as the more emotional or mental piece, these things come to mind:Sometimes a crucial part is looking at the habit of smoking and seeing what else you can do to keep your mind and hands busy. There are sometimes toys, like those available at Office Playground, that may help to keep you physically occupied.There can also be changes or additions to your routine because I imagine that smoking took up a great deal of your time. Maybe when you have the temptation to get up and go to have a cigarette, you could have several other things that you can get up and do instead. The list is endless, but a few examples could be doing some physical exercise (with the permission of your doctor) just for a couple minutes because that could help with the craving as well, completing a puzzle, learning a new activity that requires using your hands (painting or knitting, perhaps).Another piece of quitting smoking is often linked to anxiety or other emotional changes, perhaps irritability. Depending on what it is that you may be feeling,  learning other strategies to use can be helpful as well.This can all be quite overwhelming and a very big life change. I would recommend that if things do not become easier for you, consider talking with a therapist. Then you could not only have some more strategies or ideas directly related to you, but you could have very direct support for what you are trying to do.I give you a lot of credit!

question: I'm addicted to smoking. How can I stop? I'm planning to have baby, so I have to quit smoking - but it's hard. Sometimes it's not a physical need, it's mental. I cannot help myself from thinking about smoking. What can I do to get rid of this addiction?
response: Hi. Good for you in planning ahead to do what's healthiest for your baby (and yourself). That's a great first step! It's also good that you are able to identify that it's not always a physical need that's driving the addiction.For the next steps, I would suggest trying to figure out when the psychological cravings for a cigarette occur. The psychological (or mental) cravings are usually based out of habit, such as having a cigarette after a meal. And if you're consciously trying to quit, you'll find the craving starts with simply thinking about having a cigarette, then usually moves on to thinking about how good it made you feel, etc., etc. Well, if I'm on a diet and I continue to let myself think about the ice cream sitting in the freezer, eventually I'll give in and eat it.You're going to have thoughts about smoking a cigarette. That's normal and, for the most part, out of your control. But you choose whether or not to CONTINUE thinking and dwelling about it after that initial thought. That's what you would have to work on changing. When you have that initial thought, acknowledge it ("Ok, I kind of want a cigarette now."), but then change the thoughts that typically follow. Distract yourself, think about something else, do something else, whatever it takes to get your mind off of that cigarette.I've suggested to clients before that they should plan these scenarios out ahead of time so they already know what they're going to do when the time comes. Write down when you usually have the craving for a cigarette and then write down new thoughts or things to do to get your mind off of it. Eventually, it will become easier and easier to brush off that initial thought until you no longer have it.Best of luck, and you have a really great motivator to quit - your baby!

question: I'm addicted to smoking. How can I stop? I'm planning to have baby, so I have to quit smoking - but it's hard. Sometimes it's not a physical need, it's mental. I cannot help myself from thinking about smoking. What can I do to get rid of this addiction?
response:
First off, congratulations on making the decision to start a family. That is a powerful motivator, and it’s completely normal to feel like the mental "itch" is harder to scratch than the physical one. Nicotine leaves your system in a few days, but the mental habits can linger much longer.

Here is a breakdown of how to tackle both the psychological cravings and the lifestyle shifts needed to quit for good.

---

## 1. Deconstruct the "Mental" Need
When you say it's mental, you’re usually talking about **triggers**—specific times, places, or emotions that your brain has hard-wired to a cigarette.

* **The 5-Minute Rule:** Most intense cravings only last about 3 to 5 minutes. If you can distract yourself for those 300 seconds, the "need" usually subsides.
* **Identify Your "Whys":** Do you smoke when you're stressed? Bored? Finished with a meal? Once you identify the trigger, you can create a "If/Then" plan.
    * *Example:* "If I feel stressed after work, then I will listen to a 5-minute podcast instead of lighting up."

## 2. Practical Strategies for the "Mental Itch"
Since your hands and mouth are used to the habit, give them something else to do:

* **The Oral Substitute:** Keep cinnamon sticks, celery, or sugar-free gum nearby. Sometimes the brain just wants the "sensorimotor" action of smoking.
* **Deep Breathing:** Smoking forces you to take long, deep breaths. You can get a similar calming effect by inhaling deeply through a straw or just practicing "box breathing" (inhale 4s, hold 4s, exhale 4s, hold 4s).
* **Change Your Environment:** If you always smoke on the balcony, avoid the balcony for two weeks. If you smoke while drinking coffee, switch to tea for a while to break the association.

## 3. Prepare for Parenthood (The Big "Why")
Since you are planning for a baby, use that vision as an anchor:

* **The "Baby Box":** Put the money you would have spent on cigarettes into a glass jar. Watch it grow and use it specifically for something for the nursery.
* **Health Fact Check:** Remind yourself that quitting now—*before* conception—optimizes your fertility and ensures the healthiest possible environment for a developing baby. It’s the first gift you are giving your future child.

---

## 4. Professional Resources
You don't have to white-knuckle this alone. Because you are planning a pregnancy, some medications might be better than others.

| Resource Type | Benefit |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Consult your GP** | They can discuss NRT (Nicotine Replacement Therapy) like patches or gum, and ensure they are safe for your specific preconception timeline. |
| **CBT (Therapy)** | Cognitive Behavioral Therapy is excellent for "rewiring" the mental addiction and managing the anxiety that comes with quitting. |
| **Quitlines** | Calling a support line (like 1-800-QUIT-NOW) provides immediate human interaction during a craving. |

---

### A Quick Reality Check
It is a myth that you need "willpower" to be perfect. Most successful ex-smokers tried to quit several times before it stuck. If you slip up, don't throw away the whole goal. Just acknowledge the trigger and start again at the next hour.

What is the specific time of day when you find it hardest to resist the thought of smoking?

question: I'm addicted to smoking. How can I stop? I'm planning to have baby, so I have to quit smoking - but it's hard. Sometimes it's not a physical need, it's mental. I cannot help myself from thinking about smoking. What can I do to get rid of this addiction?
response:
Quitting smoking, especially when preparing to have a baby, is a significant and commendable decision. While it can be challenging, it's entirely achievable with the right strategies and resources. Here are some effective steps to help you stop smoking:

1. Build Your Motivation
Focus on your "why": Remind yourself that quitting smoking improves your health and increases your chances of having a healthy pregnancy and baby.
Make a list of benefits, such as better fertility, reduced risks of complications, and being a healthier role model.
Visualize your future—a smoke-free life for you and your child.
2. Seek Professional Support
Consult a healthcare provider: Speak with your doctor or a specialist about quitting smoking. They can provide personalized advice and help you choose the best methods.
Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT): Options like nicotine patches, gums, or lozenges can help reduce cravings while you gradually wean off nicotine. However, consult your doctor before using NRT if you're planning a pregnancy.
3. Address the Mental Aspect
Practice mindfulness: When the urge to smoke arises, pause and focus on your breathing. Acknowledge the craving without acting on it—it will pass.
Identify triggers: Keep a journal to track when and why you feel like smoking. Replace smoking with healthier habits during these moments, such as drinking water, chewing gum, or taking a walk.
Distract your mind: Engage in activities that occupy your hands or mind, like knitting, drawing, puzzles, or exercise.
4. Create a Quit Plan
Set a quit date: Pick a specific day to quit smoking entirely and prepare for it.
Remove temptations: Discard cigarettes, lighters, and ashtrays from your home, car, and workplace.
Replace smoking rituals: If you smoke with coffee, switch to tea or water. If you smoke during breaks, try stretching or chatting with a supportive friend instead.
5. Build a Support System
Tell your loved ones: Share your plan to quit smoking with friends and family. Their encouragement can help you stay accountable.
Join a support group: Look for local or online groups where you can share experiences and learn from others who are quitting.
6. Manage Stress
Practice relaxation techniques: Deep breathing, meditation, or yoga can help reduce stress and keep your mind calm.
Exercise regularly: Physical activity releases endorphins, which can improve your mood and reduce cravings.
Prioritize sleep: Good rest helps your body and mind recover, making it easier to resist urges.
7. Consider Behavioral Therapy
A counselor or therapist can help you understand the mental patterns behind your addiction and develop coping strategies to overcome them.
8. Celebrate Small Wins
Reward yourself for milestones—one day, one week, one month smoke-free.
Use the money you save from not buying cigarettes to treat yourself to something you enjoy.
9. Be Prepared for Slip-Ups
If you slip and smoke, don’t give up. Reflect on what triggered it and adjust your plan to prevent it from happening again.
10. Apps and Resources
Use apps like QuitNow, Smoke Free, or MyQuitCoach to track progress, receive motivation, and manage cravings.
Access quitline services such as the National Tobacco Quitline (1-800-QUIT-NOW) for free support.
Final Thoughts
Quitting smoking is a process, and it’s okay to seek help and take small steps. You’re not just doing this for yourself but also for your future child—and that’s a powerful motivator. Every cigarette you don’t smoke is a victory, so celebrate your progress and keep moving forward. You’ve got this!

question: I'm addicted to smoking. How can I stop? I'm planning to have baby, so I have to quit smoking - but it's hard. Sometimes it's not a physical need, it's mental. I cannot help myself from thinking about smoking. What can I do to get rid of this addiction?
response: Breaking any habit is no easy feat.   Cutting down or cutting out cigarettes is very challenging, and there aren't any one size fits all solutions.  Fortunately, there are a lot of tricks and tools that you can use to stop smoking.  1. Cutting out cigarettes will be good for you and your baby.

question: What can I do to help my dad stop drinking? Or how to send him somewhere that can help him, something like The Baker Act.
response:
Helping a loved one, like your dad, stop drinking is a challenging but compassionate goal. It’s important to approach this situation with understanding, patience, and the right resources. Let’s break this into steps:

1. Understand the Situation
Know the Scope of the Problem: Determine how severe your dad's drinking is. Is it occasional, daily, or affecting his health, relationships, or work? If it’s severe, it may require professional intervention.
Recognize Alcoholism as a Disease: Alcoholism (or Alcohol Use Disorder – AUD) is a medical condition. Stopping isn’t just about willpower; it often requires support and treatment.
2. Have a Compassionate Conversation
Choose the Right Time: Talk with your dad when he is sober, calm, and open to discussion.
Be Non-Judgmental: Use “I” statements to express your concerns. For example:
“I’m worried about your health because of how much you’re drinking.”
“I feel scared when I see how drinking is affecting you.”
Offer Support: Let him know you’re there to help, not to judge or control him.
3. Encourage Professional Help
Suggest Treatment Options: Encourage your dad to seek help from:
A doctor for medical advice.
Therapists or counselors specializing in substance abuse.
Rehabilitation centers for more intensive services.
Support groups like Alcoholics Anonymous (AA).
Share Resources: Provide him with contact information for local services.
4. Consider Involuntary Intervention (Last Resort)
If your dad’s drinking is severely endangering his health or safety (or the safety of others), involuntary intervention might be necessary. This depends on local laws and procedures:

The Baker Act (Florida): This law allows for involuntary psychiatric evaluation if someone is a danger to themselves or others due to mental illness. However, substance abuse alone may not qualify unless it’s accompanied by mental health issues.
The Marchman Act (Florida): This law specifically allows for the involuntary assessment and treatment of individuals with substance abuse issues. You can petition the court for this if your dad is unable to make rational decisions about his own care and is at risk.
If you are in another state or country, research the relevant laws or talk to a lawyer or social worker to explore similar options.

5. Take Care of Yourself
Supporting a loved one with addiction can take a toll on your own mental and emotional health. Make sure you:

Seek support for yourself through Al-Anon or similar groups for families of alcoholics.
Set boundaries to protect your own well-being.
Reach out to a therapist or counselor for guidance.
6. Find Local Resources
Here are ways to locate help:

SAMHSA’s Helpline (U.S.): Call 1-800-662-HELP (4357) for confidential, free information and referrals to treatment facilities.
Substance Abuse Treatment Locator: Visit findtreatment.gov.
Talk to a Social Worker: They can help you navigate local resources.
Final Notes
You can’t force your dad to change, but you can provide support, encouragement, and options for help. If he refuses, focus on ensuring his safety and your own mental health, and don’t hesitate to seek professional guidance for the next steps.
